input.plan:
# PostHog Integration Plan

## Context

Kapow has zero analytics or error tracking. This plan adds PostHog to the web
frontend for error tracking and product analytics, while architecting the
provider hierarchy so session replay, experiments, and feature flags can be
enabled later without structural changes.

## Approach

Follow the existing `supabase.ts` pattern: a library module that gracefully
degrades to no-ops when env vars are missing. Wrap the app with
`PostHogProvider` from `posthog-js/react` so the `usePostHog()` hook and
future feature-flag hooks work throughout the tree.

## New dependency

- `posthog-js` (includes `posthog-js/react` — provider, hooks)

## Environment variables

| Var | Required | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` | Yes (to enable) | — |
| `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` | No | `https://us.i.posthog.com` |

When `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` is absent, the entire integration is inert.

## Files to create

### 1. `web/src/lib/posthog.ts` — Client initialization

- Read `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` / `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` from `import.meta.env`
- Export `posthogEnabled: boolean`
- If enabled: `posthog.init(key, { api_host, capture_pageview: false, capture_pageleave: true, autocapture: false, respect_dnt: true, persistence: 'localStorage' })`
- Register `window.onerror` / `window.onunhandledrejection` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Export the posthog instance
- If disabled: export a no-op proxy (mirrors supabase.ts pattern)

### 2. `web/src/hooks/usePostHog.ts` — Pageview + identify hooks

- `usePostHogPageview()`: captures `$pageview` on `location.pathname` changes via `useLocation()`
- `usePostHogIdentify()`: subscribes to `useAuthStore`, calls `posthog.identify()` / `posthog.reset()`
- Both are no-ops when `posthogEnabled` is false

### 3. `web/src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx` — React error boundary

- Class component, `componentDidCatch` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Fallback UI using Mantine components (title, message, reload button)

### 4. `web/src/lib/posthogExchange.ts` — urql exchange

- Inspects operation results for `graphQLErrors`
- Captures `graphql_error` events with operation name + error messages
- Passthrough when PostHog is disabled

## Files to modify

### 5. `web/src/main.tsx` — Provider + error boundary wiring

```
StrictMode > UrqlProvider > AppProviders > PostHogProvider > ErrorBoundary > BrowserRouter > App
```

### 6. `web/src/App.tsx` — Wire hooks

- Call `usePostHogPageview()` and `usePostHogIdentify()` in App component

### 7. `web/src/lib/urqlClient.ts` — Add exchange

- Insert `posthogExchange` into exchanges: `[cacheExchange, posthogExchange, authExchange(...), fetchExchange]`

### 8. `web/src/pages/Search.tsx` — Custom analytics events

- `search_executed` — on search results load (filter count, result count, has_location)
- `result_clicked` — on institution click (unit_id, name)
- `export_csv` / `export_pdf` — on export button clicks

### 9. `.env.example` — Document new env vars

### 10. `web/src/test/setup.ts` — Global mocks

- Mock `posthog-js` and `posthog-js/react` with no-op stubs

## PostHog init config

```typescript
posthog.init(key, {
  api_host: host || 'https://us.i.posthog.com',
  capture_pageview: false,       // manual via hook
  capture_pageleave: true,
  autocapture: false,            // explicit events only
  respect_dnt: true,
  persistence: 'localStorage',
  loaded: (ph) => { if (import.meta.env.DEV) ph.debug() },
})
```

## Implementation order

1. Install `posthog-js` dependency
2. `src/lib/posthog.ts` — foundation module
3. `src/test/setup.ts` — add mocks (all subsequent tests pass)
4. `src/lib/posthogExchange.ts`
5. `src/hooks/usePostHog.ts`
6. `src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx`
7. `src/lib/urqlClient.ts` — wire exchange
8. `src/main.tsx` — wire provider + error boundary
9. `src/App.tsx` — wire hooks
10. `src/pages/Search.tsx` — custom events
11. `.env.example` — document vars
12. Tests for new files
13. `pnpm build && pnpm lint` — verify clean

## What this intentionally skips

- **Session replay** — enable later by removing `disable_session_recording: true`
- **Feature flags / experiments** — work automatically once enabled in PostHog dashboard; `PostHogProvider` is already in the tree
- **Server-side tracking** — separate concern for the Go API
- **Cookie consent** — US-focused tool; `respect_dnt: true` is sufficient

file.content:
# Agent Instructions

This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.

## Quick Reference

```bash
bd ready              # Find available work
bd show <id>          # View issue details
bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
bd close <id>         # Complete work
bd sync               # Sync with git
```

## Issue Title Guidelines

Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:

| Bad | Good |
|---|---|
| `Add support for filtering by SAT score` | `SAT score filter for search` |
| `Update the search page to show result counts` | `Search result count display` |
| `Fix bug where geographic radius is ignored` | `Geo radius filter ignored` |
| `Implement new card component for institutions` | `Institution card component` |

- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Search …", "Auth …", "GeoField …"), not filler verbs
- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"

## Creating Issues

Use the correct issue type:
- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability
- `task` — scaffold, chore, or infrastructure work (e.g., project init, CI setup)
- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing

### Creating Epics

Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:

```bash
bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
  -d "Description of the epic" \
  --waits-for-gate all-children
```

This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.

### Creating Child Issues Under Epics

Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:

```bash
bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l search,go \
  --parent kapow-abc \
  --waits-for kapow-abc \
  -d "Description" \
  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
```

The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for kapow-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.

### Dependencies Between Issues

Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:

```bash
bd dep add kapow-child kapow-dependency   # kapow-child is blocked by kapow-dependency
```

Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the migration must exist before the resolver can query the new column"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.

## Implementation Plans

Claude Code saves plans to `~/.claude/plans/` with random names (e.g.,
`replicated-drifting-meerkat.md`). These are local to the machine and lost
when switching devices. **After a plan is approved, copy it into the repo**
so it persists across machines and sessions.

### Saving a plan to the repo

After exiting plan mode, copy the plan file from `~/.claude/plans/` into
`docs/plans/`, renaming it to something meaningful:

```bash
cp ~/.claude/plans/<random-name>.md docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md
```

Examples:
- `docs/plans/kapow-a1b-geo-search.md`
- `docs/plans/auth-refactor.md` (if no issue exists yet)

Use lowercase kebab-case. Prefer `<issue-id>-<short-name>` when an issue exists.

### Referencing from issues

Add a note to the beads issue pointing to the plan:

content.content:
100→
   101→```bash
   102→bd update <id> --notes="Plan: docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md"
   103→```
   104→
   105→### Resuming work from a plan
   106→
   107→When starting a new session, check `docs/plans/` for in-progress plans.
   108→If a beads issue references a plan file, read it before starting work.
   109→
   110→### Lifecycle
   111→
   112→- Commit and push plan files alongside the code they describe
   113→- Update plan files as the approach evolves during implementation
   114→- Delete plan files after the work is fully merged and closed
   115→
   116→## Completing an Issue
   117→
   118→An issue is not complete until:
   119→1. All code changes pass the required quality gate (see below)
   120→2. Changes are committed
   121→3. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
   122→4. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`
   123→
   124→## Quality Gates (Mandatory)
   125→
   126→Every code change MUST pass the appropriate quality gate **before committing**.
   127→Do not skip gates, use `--no-verify`, or treat failures as acceptable.
   128→
   129→### Required gate by change type
   130→
   131→| Change type | Gate | Command |
   132→|---|---|---|
   133→| Go code only | Standard | `make test-standard` |
   134→| Web code only | Standard | `make test-standard` |
   135→| Go + Web | Standard | `make test-standard` |
   136→| GraphQL schema (`.graphql`) | Standard + regenerate | `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync && make test-standard` |
   137→| Database migration | Full | `make test-full` |
   138→| Fixture data | Fixture | `make test-fixture` |
   139→| Docs only | Quick | `make test-quick` |
   140→
   141→**Rules:**
   142→- The gate must exit with **zero errors and zero warnings**
   143→- Run the gate from the repo root, not a subdirectory
   144→- If your change spans multiple types, use the **strictest** gate
   145→- When in doubt, run `make test-standard` — it covers most cases
   146→
   147→### Responding to gate failures
   148→
   149→1. **Read the full error output** — identify the root cause, not just the first line
   150→2. **Fix the code, not the test** — unless the test itself is wrong
   151→3. **Re-run the complete gate command** — partial re-runs miss cascading failures
   152→4. **If the failure is in generated code**, regenerate first: `make api-generate` and/or `make web-schema-sync`
   153→5. **Never bypass**: no `--no-verify`, no `--force`, no skipping the gate
   154→
   155→## Contract Test Rules
   156→
   157→The search validation contract (`api/internal/search/contract.go`) defines a
   158→shared test matrix consumed by three entry-point test runners:
   159→
   160→- `api/internal/search/contract_test.go` — baseline (ground truth)
   161→- `api/internal/handler/contract_test.go` — CSV export adapter
   162→- `api/internal/mcp/contract_test.go` — MCP protocol adapter
   163→
   164→### When you MUST update `ContractCases()`
   165→
   166→- Adding, removing, or renaming a search field
   167→- Changing validation logic in `ValidateRequest`
   168→- Adding a new entry point that consumes `SearchRequest`
   169→- Changing auth requirements for search fields
   170→
   171→### Contract update workflow
   172→
   173→1. Edit `api/internal/search/contract.go` — add/modify cases
   174→2. Run `make api-test` — all three contract runners must pass
   175→3. If a new entry point is added, create a corresponding `contract_test.go` that
   176→   consumes `ContractCases()` with the same assertion pattern
   177→
   178→**All three contract test files must pass.** A change that passes in one runner
   179→but fails in another indicates contract drift — fix the adapter, not the contract.
   180→
   181→## Protected Surfaces
   182→
   183→These areas require extra verification beyond the standard gate. Changes to
   184→protected surfaces that skip the additional checks MUST be rejected.
   185→
   186→| Surface | Extra requirement | Why |
   187→|---|---|---|
   188→| Generated files (`graph/generated/`, `graph/model/`, `web/src/graphql-env.d.ts`) | Never edit manually; always regenerate with `make api-generate` / `make web-schema-sync` | Manual edits are silently overwritten on next generate |
   189→| Migration files (`api/migrations/`) | `make test-full` with live DB; update fixtures if schema changes | Runtime pgx type errors only surface with a live connection |
   190→| Search field registry (`api/internal/search/registry.go`) | Update contract cases; verify `TestBuildSQLAllFields` passes | Contract drift across entry points |
   191→| Auth middleware (`api/internal/middleware/auth.go`) | Integration tests with real JWT validation | Auth bugs are silent and security-critical |
   192→| GraphQL schema (`api/graph/schema/*.graphql`) | `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync` before any other checks | Stale generated code causes confusing type errors |
   193→| Fixture data (`common/src/main/resources/data/`) | `make test-fixture` | Fixture/schema mismatch breaks all integration tests |
   194→
   195→## Git Workflow
   196→
   197→When work is complete and verified (tests pass, build succeeds, lint is clean),
   198→merge directly into `main`. Only fall back to a pull request if there is an
   199→unresolvable problem merging from the command line (e.g. complex conflicts).
   200→
   201→### Commit messages and issue references
   202→
   203→When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files), list all
   204→affected issue IDs in the commit message body. This makes it easy to trace
   205→which issues were created, updated, or closed in each push.
   206→
   207→Example:
   208→```
   209→Add geo search field and update filter UI
   210→
   211→Implements proximity-based search using PostGIS ST_DWithin.
   212→
   213→Issues: kapow-a1b, kapow-c2d, kapow-e3f
   214→```
   215→
   216→### Worktree workflow
   217→
   218→When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
   219→the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
   220→worktree branch unmerged without asking.
   221→
   222→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   223→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   224→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)
   225→
   226→**After merging a worktree that touches GraphQL schema files:**
   227→- Always regenerate: `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
   228→- `.gitattributes` marks generated files with `merge=ours` — conflicts auto-resolve to the current branch, but you must regenerate to get the correct content
   229→
   230→**Worktree cleanup — never for Claude-managed worktrees:**
   231→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically — both for `--worktree`
   232→sessions and for subagents using `isolation: "worktree"`. These worktrees are
   233→cleaned up on session/agent exit.
   234→
   235→**Rules:**
   236→- **Never** run `rm -rf` on any worktree directory — use `git worktree remove` instead, which properly updates git bookkeeping
   237→- **Never** run `git worktree remove` on a Claude-managed worktree (`.claude/worktrees/`) — let the session end naturally; manual removal mid-session breaks the working directory
   238→- `git worktree remove` is fine for non-Claude worktrees (e.g. user-created worktrees that need cleanup)
   239→
   240→## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
   241→
   242→**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
   243→
   244→**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
   245→
   246→1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
   247→2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
   248→3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
   249→4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   250→   ```bash
   251→   git pull --rebase
   252→   bd sync
   253→   git push
   254→   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   255→   ```
   256→5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
   257→6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
   258→7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
   259→
   260→**CRITICAL RULES:**
   261→- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
   262→- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
   263→- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
   264→- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
   265→
   266→
   267→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   268→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   269→
   270→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   271→
   272→### Why bd?
   273→
   274→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   275→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
   276→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   277→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   278→
   279→### Quick Start
   280→
   281→**Check for ready work:**
   282→
   283→```bash
   284→bd ready --json
   285→```
   286→
   287→**Create new issues:**
   288→
   289→```bash
   290→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
   291→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json

file.content:
```bash
bd update <id> --notes="Plan: docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md"
```

### Resuming work from a plan

When starting a new session, check `docs/plans/` for in-progress plans.
If a beads issue references a plan file, read it before starting work.

### Lifecycle

- Commit and push plan files alongside the code they describe
- Update plan files as the approach evolves during implementation
- Delete plan files after the work is fully merged and closed

## Completing an Issue

An issue is not complete until:
1. All code changes pass the required quality gate (see below)
2. Changes are committed
3. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
4. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`

## Quality Gates (Mandatory)

Every code change MUST pass the appropriate quality gate **before committing**.
Do not skip gates, use `--no-verify`, or treat failures as acceptable.

### Required gate by change type

| Change type | Gate | Command |
|---|---|---|
| Go code only | Standard | `make test-standard` |
| Web code only | Standard | `make test-standard` |
| Go + Web | Standard | `make test-standard` |
| GraphQL schema (`.graphql`) | Standard + regenerate | `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync && make test-standard` |
| Database migration | Full | `make test-full` |
| Fixture data | Fixture | `make test-fixture` |
| Docs only | Quick | `make test-quick` |

**Rules:**
- The gate must exit with **zero errors and zero warnings**
- Run the gate from the repo root, not a subdirectory
- If your change spans multiple types, use the **strictest** gate
- When in doubt, run `make test-standard` — it covers most cases

### Responding to gate failures

1. **Read the full error output** — identify the root cause, not just the first line
2. **Fix the code, not the test** — unless the test itself is wrong
3. **Re-run the complete gate command** — partial re-runs miss cascading failures
4. **If the failure is in generated code**, regenerate first: `make api-generate` and/or `make web-schema-sync`
5. **Never bypass**: no `--no-verify`, no `--force`, no skipping the gate

## Contract Test Rules

The search validation contract (`api/internal/search/contract.go`) defines a
shared test matrix consumed by three entry-point test runners:

- `api/internal/search/contract_test.go` — baseline (ground truth)
- `api/internal/handler/contract_test.go` — CSV export adapter
- `api/internal/mcp/contract_test.go` — MCP protocol adapter

### When you MUST update `ContractCases()`

- Adding, removing, or renaming a search field
- Changing validation logic in `ValidateRequest`
- Adding a new entry point that consumes `SearchRequest`
- Changing auth requirements for search fields

### Contract update workflow

1. Edit `api/internal/search/contract.go` — add/modify cases
2. Run `make api-test` — all three contract runners must pass
3. If a new entry point is added, create a corresponding `contract_test.go` that
   consumes `ContractCases()` with the same assertion pattern

**All three contract test files must pass.** A change that passes in one runner
but fails in another indicates contract drift — fix the adapter, not the contract.

## Protected Surfaces

These areas require extra verification beyond the standard gate. Changes to
protected surfaces that skip the additional checks MUST be rejected.

| Surface | Extra requirement | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Generated files (`graph/generated/`, `graph/model/`, `web/src/graphql-env.d.ts`) | Never edit manually; always regenerate with `make api-generate` / `make web-schema-sync` | Manual edits are silently overwritten on next generate |
| Migration files (`api/migrations/`) | `make test-full` with live DB; update fixtures if schema changes | Runtime pgx type errors only surface with a live connection |
| Search field registry (`api/internal/search/registry.go`) | Update contract cases; verify `TestBuildSQLAllFields` passes | Contract drift across entry points |
| Auth middleware (`api/internal/middleware/auth.go`) | Integration tests with real JWT validation | Auth bugs are silent and security-critical |
| GraphQL schema (`api/graph/schema/*.graphql`) | `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync` before any other checks | Stale generated code causes confusing type errors |
| Fixture data (`common/src/main/resources/data/`) | `make test-fixture` | Fixture/schema mismatch breaks all integration tests |

## Git Workflow

When work is complete and verified (tests pass, build succeeds, lint is clean),
merge directly into `main`. Only fall back to a pull request if there is an
unresolvable problem merging from the command line (e.g. complex conflicts).

### Commit messages and issue references

When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files), list all
affected issue IDs in the commit message body. This makes it easy to trace
which issues were created, updated, or closed in each push.

Example:
```
Add geo search field and update filter UI

Implements proximity-based search using PostGIS ST_DWithin.

Issues: kapow-a1b, kapow-c2d, kapow-e3f
```

### Worktree workflow

When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
worktree branch unmerged without asking.

**Before merging a worktree branch:**
- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)

**After merging a worktree that touches GraphQL schema files:**
- Always regenerate: `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
- `.gitattributes` marks generated files with `merge=ours` — conflicts auto-resolve to the current branch, but you must regenerate to get the correct content

**Worktree cleanup — never for Claude-managed worktrees:**
Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically — both for `--worktree`
sessions and for subagents using `isolation: "worktree"`. These worktrees are
cleaned up on session/agent exit.

**Rules:**
- **Never** run `rm -rf` on any worktree directory — use `git worktree remove` instead, which properly updates git bookkeeping
- **Never** run `git worktree remove` on a Claude-managed worktree (`.claude/worktrees/`) — let the session end naturally; manual removal mid-session breaks the working directory
- `git worktree remove` is fine for non-Claude worktrees (e.g. user-created worktrees that need cleanup)

## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)

**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.

**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**

1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   ```bash
   git pull --rebase
   bd sync
   git push
   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   ```
5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session

**CRITICAL RULES:**
- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds


<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)

**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.

### Why bd?

- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion

### Quick Start

**Check for ready work:**

```bash
bd ready --json
```

**Create new issues:**

```bash
bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
```

**Claim and update:**

```bash
bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
```

**Complete work:**

```bash
bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
```

### Issue Types

- `bug` - Something broken
- `feature` - New functionality
- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)

### Priorities

- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)

### Workflow for AI Agents

1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`

### Auto-Sync

bd automatically syncs with git:

- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
- No manual export/import needed!

### Important Rules

- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems

For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.

<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->

file.content:
# PostHog Integration Plan

## Context

Kapow has zero analytics or error tracking. This plan adds PostHog to the web
frontend for error tracking and product analytics, while architecting the
provider hierarchy so session replay, experiments, and feature flags can be
enabled later without structural changes.

## Approach

Follow the existing `supabase.ts` pattern: a library module that gracefully
degrades to no-ops when env vars are missing. Wrap the app with
`PostHogProvider` from `posthog-js/react` so the `usePostHog()` hook and
future feature-flag hooks work throughout the tree.

## New dependency

- `posthog-js` (includes `posthog-js/react` — provider, hooks)

## Environment variables

| Var | Required | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` | Yes (to enable) | — |
| `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` | No | `https://us.i.posthog.com` |

When `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` is absent, the entire integration is inert.

## Files to create

### 1. `web/src/lib/posthog.ts` — Client initialization

- Read `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` / `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` from `import.meta.env`
- Export `posthogEnabled: boolean`
- If enabled: `posthog.init(key, { api_host, capture_pageview: false, capture_pageleave: true, autocapture: false, respect_dnt: true, persistence: 'localStorage' })`
- Register `window.onerror` / `window.onunhandledrejection` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Export the posthog instance
- If disabled: export a no-op proxy (mirrors supabase.ts pattern)

### 2. `web/src/hooks/usePostHog.ts` — Pageview + identify hooks

- `usePostHogPageview()`: captures `$pageview` on `location.pathname` changes via `useLocation()`
- `usePostHogIdentify()`: subscribes to `useAuthStore`, calls `posthog.identify()` / `posthog.reset()`
- Both are no-ops when `posthogEnabled` is false

### 3. `web/src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx` — React error boundary

- Class component, `componentDidCatch` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Fallback UI using Mantine components (title, message, reload button)

### 4. `web/src/lib/posthogExchange.ts` — urql exchange

- Inspects operation results for `graphQLErrors`
- Captures `graphql_error` events with operation name + error messages
- Passthrough when PostHog is disabled

## Files to modify

### 5. `web/src/main.tsx` — Provider + error boundary wiring

```
StrictMode > UrqlProvider > AppProviders > PostHogProvider > ErrorBoundary > BrowserRouter > App
```

### 6. `web/src/App.tsx` — Wire hooks

- Call `usePostHogPageview()` and `usePostHogIdentify()` in App component

### 7. `web/src/lib/urqlClient.ts` — Add exchange

- Insert `posthogExchange` into exchanges: `[cacheExchange, posthogExchange, authExchange(...), fetchExchange]`

### 8. `web/src/pages/Search.tsx` — Custom analytics events

- `search_executed` — on search results load (filter count, result count, has_location)
- `result_clicked` — on institution click (unit_id, name)
- `export_csv` / `export_pdf` — on export button clicks

### 9. `.env.example` — Document new env vars

### 10. `web/src/test/setup.ts` — Global mocks

- Mock `posthog-js` and `posthog-js/react` with no-op stubs

## PostHog init config

```typescript
posthog.init(key, {
  api_host: host || 'https://us.i.posthog.com',
  capture_pageview: false,       // manual via hook
  capture_pageleave: true,
  autocapture: false,            // explicit events only
  respect_dnt: true,
  persistence: 'localStorage',
  loaded: (ph) => { if (import.meta.env.DEV) ph.debug() },
})
```

## Implementation order

1. Install `posthog-js` dependency
2. `src/lib/posthog.ts` — foundation module
3. `src/test/setup.ts` — add mocks (all subsequent tests pass)
4. `src/lib/posthogExchange.ts`
5. `src/hooks/usePostHog.ts`
6. `src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx`
7. `src/lib/urqlClient.ts` — wire exchange
8. `src/main.tsx` — wire provider + error boundary
9. `src/App.tsx` — wire hooks
10. `src/pages/Search.tsx` — custom events
11. `.env.example` — document vars
12. Tests for new files
13. `pnpm build && pnpm lint` — verify clean

## What this intentionally skips

- **Session replay** — enable later by removing `disable_session_recording: true`
- **Feature flags / experiments** — work automatically once enabled in PostHog dashboard; `PostHogProvider` is already in the tree
- **Server-side tracking** — separate concern for the Go API
- **Cookie consent** — US-focused tool; `respect_dnt: true` is sufficient

# PostHog Integration Plan

## Context

Kapow has zero analytics or error tracking. This plan adds PostHog to the web
frontend for error tracking and product analytics, while architecting the
provider hierarchy so session replay, experiments, and feature flags can be
enabled later without structural changes.

## Approach

Follow the existing `supabase.ts` pattern: a library module that gracefully
degrades to no-ops when env vars are missing. Wrap the app with
`PostHogProvider` from `posthog-js/react` so the `usePostHog()` hook and
future feature-flag hooks work throughout the tree.

## New dependency

- `posthog-js` (includes `posthog-js/react` — provider, hooks)

## Environment variables

| Var | Required | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` | Yes (to enable) | — |
| `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` | No | `https://us.i.posthog.com` |

When `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` is absent, the entire integration is inert.

## Files to create

### 1. `web/src/lib/posthog.ts` — Client initialization

- Read `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` / `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` from `import.meta.env`
- Export `posthogEnabled: boolean`
- If enabled: `posthog.init(key, { api_host, capture_pageview: false, capture_pageleave: true, autocapture: false, respect_dnt: true, persistence: 'localStorage' })`
- Register `window.onerror` / `window.onunhandledrejection` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Export the posthog instance
- If disabled: export a no-op proxy (mirrors supabase.ts pattern)

### 2. `web/src/hooks/usePostHog.ts` — Pageview + identify hooks

- `usePostHogPageview()`: captures `$pageview` on `location.pathname` changes via `useLocation()`
- `usePostHogIdentify()`: subscribes to `useAuthStore`, calls `posthog.identify()` / `posthog.reset()`
- Both are no-ops when `posthogEnabled` is false

### 3. `web/src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx` — React error boundary

- Class component, `componentDidCatch` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Fallback UI using Mantine components (title, message, reload button)

### 4. `web/src/lib/posthogExchange.ts` — urql exchange

- Inspects operation results for `graphQLErrors`
- Captures `graphql_error` events with operation name + error messages
- Passthrough when PostHog is disabled

## Files to modify

### 5. `web/src/main.tsx` — Provider + error boundary wiring

```
StrictMode > UrqlProvider > AppProviders > PostHogProvider > ErrorBoundary > BrowserRouter > App
```

### 6. `web/src/App.tsx` — Wire hooks

- Call `usePostHogPageview()` and `usePostHogIdentify()` in App component

### 7. `web/src/lib/urqlClient.ts` — Add exchange

- Insert `posthogExchange` into exchanges: `[cacheExchange, posthogExchange, authExchange(...), fetchExchange]`

### 8. `web/src/pages/Search.tsx` — Custom analytics events

- `search_executed` — on search results load (filter count, result count, has_location)
- `result_clicked` — on institution click (unit_id, name)
- `export_csv` / `export_pdf` — on export button clicks

### 9. `.env.example` — Document new env vars

### 10. `web/src/test/setup.ts` — Global mocks

- Mock `posthog-js` and `posthog-js/react` with no-op stubs

## PostHog init config

```typescript
posthog.init(key, {
  api_host: host || 'https://us.i.posthog.com',
  capture_pageview: false,       // manual via hook
  capture_pageleave: true,
  autocapture: false,            // explicit events only
  respect_dnt: true,
  persistence: 'localStorage',
  loaded: (ph) => { if (import.meta.env.DEV) ph.debug() },
})
```

## Implementation order

1. Install `posthog-js` dependency
2. `src/lib/posthog.ts` — foundation module
3. `src/test/setup.ts` — add mocks (all subsequent tests pass)
4. `src/lib/posthogExchange.ts`
5. `src/hooks/usePostHog.ts`
6. `src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx`
7. `src/lib/urqlClient.ts` — wire exchange
8. `src/main.tsx` — wire provider + error boundary
9. `src/App.tsx` — wire hooks
10. `src/pages/Search.tsx` — custom events
11. `.env.example` — document vars
12. Tests for new files
13. `pnpm build && pnpm lint` — verify clean

## What this intentionally skips

- **Session replay** — enable later by removing `disable_session_recording: true`
- **Feature flags / experiments** — work automatically once enabled in PostHog dashboard; `PostHogProvider` is already in the tree
- **Server-side tracking** — separate concern for the Go API
- **Cookie consent** — US-focused tool; `respect_dnt: true` is sufficient

lines: +| `kapow-d1i.1` | PostHog client module + test mocks | — | P1 |
+| `kapow-d1i.2` | React error boundary + PostHog exceptions | .1 | P1 |
+**Parallelism**: After `.1` completes, `.2`–`.5` can all run in parallel. `.6` is the final assembly.
- **Session replay** — enable later by removing `disable_session_recording: true`

content.text:
Good question. PostHog is primarily a product analytics tool — it handles frontend errors and user behavior well, but it's not designed for server-side observability like:

- **Go panic/error tracking** with stack traces and breadcrumbs
- **Query execution times** (P50/P95/P99)
- **Request latency** by endpoint
- **Database connection pool stats**

For server-side, the options are:

| Approach | Error tracking | Operational metrics | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PostHog Go SDK only** | Basic (events, no stack traces) | No | Low |
| **Sentry Go SDK** | Excellent (panics, stack traces, breadcrumbs, performance spans) | Request/query durations via tracing | Medium |
| **OpenTelemetry → PostHog/Sentry** | Via exporter | Full traces + metrics | High |
| **Structured logging + slog** (already in place) | Log-based (grep for errors) | Can log query times | Zero (extend existing) |

My recommendation: **Add Sentry for the Go API** alongside PostHog for the frontend. Sentry excels at server-side error tracking with proper Go panic recovery and has built-in performance monitoring that can capture query times. PostHog handles product analytics; Sentry handles operational reliability. They complement rather than overlap.

Alternatively, if you want to keep it to one vendor, we could use PostHog's Go SDK for basic server-side error events and add manual `slog` timing for query metrics — simpler but less powerful.

What's your preference?

Links: [{"title":"Error tracking - Docs - PostHog","url":"https://posthog.com/docs/error-tracking"},{"title":"Error Tracking – Catch and fix issues faster with PostHog","url":"https://posthog.com/error-tracking"},{"title":"Error Tracking - PostHog","url":"https://archive.posthog.com/error-tracking"},{"title":"Error Tracking Support · Issue #95 · PostHog/posthog-go","url":"https://github.com/PostHog/posthog-go/issues/95"},{"title":"JavaScript web - Docs - PostHog","url":"https://posthog.com/docs/libraries/js"},{"title":"posthog package - github.com/posthog/posthog-go - Go Packages","url":"https://pkg.go.dev/github.com/posthog/posthog-go"},{"title":"Error Tracking troubleshooting - Docs - PostHog","url":"https://posthog.com/docs/error-tracking/troubleshooting"},{"title":"Install error tracking with PostHog - Docs - PostHog","url":"https://posthog.com/docs/error-tracking/installation"},{"title":"Getting started with Error Tracking - Docs - PostHog","url":"https://posthog.com/docs/error-tracking/start-here"},{"title":"Capture exceptions for error tracking - Docs - PostHog","url":"https://posthog.com/docs/error-tracking/capture"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about PostHog error tracking for Go backend server-side:

## PostHog Error Tracking Support for Go

The PostHog Go SDK supports capturing errors/exceptions using methods like `client.Enqueue(posthog.NewDefaultException(...))`, which allows you to capture error titles and descriptions from your backend.

Additionally, the SDK includes a logger integration that automatically captures warning logs and above through the `posthog.NewSlogCaptureHandler` method, enabling automatic error tracking when using Go's structured logging.

## Current Status

Here are the search results for PostHog OpenTelemetry logs integration with Go backend setup:

## Key Findings:

**Core Integration Approach:**
PostHog logs works with any OpenTelemetry-compatible client and you don't need any PostHog-specific packages - just use standard OpenTelemetry libraries.

**Setup Process:**
The process involves using standard OpenTelemetry logging APIs in your application, including your project token in the Authorization header or as a query parameter, and configuring your OpenTelemetry client to send logs to PostHog's HTTP endpoint.

**Technical Details:**
- PostHog logs uses the OpenTelemetry Protocol (OTLP) standard
- Use the HTTP endpoint: https://us.i.posthog.com/i/v1/logs and authenticate with your project token (the same token used for events/exceptions)
- There are no proprietary SDKs and no new instrumentation model to learn. If you're already emitting logs via OpenTelemetry, you can send them to PostHog with minimal configuration

**Go-Specific Documentation:**
The search results confirm there is a dedicated Go logs installation page in PostHog's documentation (posthog.com/docs/logs/installation/go), which provides specific instructions for setting up the OpenTelemetry SDK to send logs to PostHog for Go backends.

For detailed Go implementation examples, you can refer directly to the PostHog documentation page for Go.

I've completed the web search for "PostHog Go SDK features error tracking logs posthog-go". Here are the key findings:

## PostHog Go SDK Error Tracking Features

**Log Capture Integration:**
The SDK provides a SlogCaptureHandler that wraps Go's standard log/slog logger and automatically captures log records as exceptions, capturing logs at Warning level and above by default.

**Exception Capture Methods:**
You can capture exceptions and errors using the Go SDK through two approaches: Direct capture using NewDefaultException, which automatically generates a stack trace, and automatic capture using the SlogCaptureHandler, which wraps Go's log/slog and sends log records at warning level and above as exceptions.

**Stack Trace Support:**
The Go SDK captures stack traces with file names, line numbers, and function names.

**Callback Interface:**
You can use the Callback interface to track successes and failures of events delivered to PostHog.

**General Error Tracking Benefits:**
Error tracking enables you to capture, monitor, and resolve exceptions within your app, and connect issues to user data and product context for faster, more effective debugging.

The search results show that PostHog's Go SDK has solid error tracking capabilities, particularly through its slog integration for automatic log-based exception capture.

content.content:
# Go Error Tracking Support Status

**Current Status: Not Implemented**

The PostHog Go SDK does not currently support error tracking. According to a contributor's response: "we don't currently support golang in our error tracking product."

**Key Points:**

- **Confirmed Gap**: The feature request was opened April 23, 2025, and closed April 25, 2025 after being documented as a feature request in the main PostHog repository (issue #31613).

- **Team Awareness**: The PostHog error tracking team was notified of the request, and the community was directed to monitor the official roadmap for planned platform support.

- **No Comparison Data**: The issue discussion does not provide any detailed comparison with Sentry or outline specific limitations beyond the absence of Go support itself.

**Recommendation**: Users needing Go error tracking with PostHog should track the main repository's feature request issue for updates on implementation timelines.

content.content:
# PostHog Go SDK - API Surface Analysis

**Version:** `v1.11.1` (Published: Mar 11, 2026)

## Core Client Interface

```go
type Client interface {
    io.Closer
    EnqueueClient
    
    IsFeatureEnabled(FeatureFlagPayload) (interface{}, error)
    GetFeatureFlag(FeatureFlagPayload) (interface{}, error)
    GetFeatureFlagResult(FeatureFlagPayload) (*FeatureFlagResult, error)
    GetFeatureFlagPayload(FeatureFlagPayload) (string, error)
    GetRemoteConfigPayload(string) (string, error)
    GetAllFlags(FeatureFlagPayloadNoKey) (map[string]interface{}, error)
    ReloadFeatureFlags() error
    GetFeatureFlags() ([]FeatureFlag, error)
    CloseWithContext(context.Context) error
}

type EnqueueClient interface {
    Enqueue(Message) error
}

// Constructors
func New(apiKey string) Client
func NewWithConfig(apiKey string, config Config) (Client, error)
```

## Message Types

All implement the `Message` interface with `Validate()` and `APIfy()` methods:

- **`Capture`** - Event tracking with optional feature flag delivery
- **`Identify`** - User property tracking
- **`Alias`** - Link user identities
- **`GroupIdentify`** - Group property tracking
- **`Exception`** - Error/exception tracking

## Error Tracking & Logging

### Exception Type (v1.6.7+)
```go
type Exception struct {
    Type                 string
    Uuid                 string
    DistinctId           string
    Timestamp            time.Time
    DisableGeoIP         bool
    ExceptionList        []ExceptionItem
    ExceptionFingerprint *string
}

// Constructor with sensible defaults
func NewDefaultException(
    timestamp time.Time,
    distinctID, title, description string,
) Exception
```

### SlogCaptureHandler (v1.6.7+)
Automatically captures logs via Go's `slog` package:

```go
type SlogCaptureHandler struct { /* ... */ }

func NewSlogCaptureHandler(
    next slog.Handler,
    client EnqueueClient,
    opts ...SlogOption,
) *SlogCaptureHandler

// Methods
func (h *SlogCaptureHandler) Handle(ctx context.Context, r slog.Record) error
func (h *SlogCaptureHandler) Enabled(ctx context.Context, level slog.Level) bool
func (h *SlogCaptureHandler) WithAttrs(attrs []slog.Attr) slog.Handler
func (h *SlogCaptureHandler) WithGroup(name string) slog.Handler
```

**SlogOptions:**
- `WithMinCaptureLevel(slog.Level)` - Minimum level to capture
- `WithDistinctIDFn(func(ctx, slog.Record) string)` - Extract user ID
- `WithFingerprintFn(func(ctx, slog.Record) *string)` - Error deduplication
- `WithStackTraceExtractor(StackTraceExtractor)` - Custom stack trace extraction
- `WithDescriptionExtractor(DescriptionExtractor)` - Custom error message extraction
- `WithSkip(int)` - Frame skip count

### Example Usage
```go
baseLogHandler := slog.NewTextHandler(os.Stdout, &slog.HandlerOptions{Level: slog.LevelInfo})
logger := slog.New(posthog.NewSlogCaptureHandler(
    baseLogHandler, client,
    posthog.WithMinCaptureLevel(slog.LevelWarn),
    posthog.WithDistinctIDFn(func(ctx context.Context, r slog.Record) string {
        return "my-user-id"
    }),
))
logger.Warn("Log that something broke", "error", fmt.Errorf("this is a dummy scenario"))
```

## Error Types

- **`APIError`** - HTTP errors with status codes
- **`ConfigError`** - Invalid configuration
- **`FieldError`** - Missing/invalid message fields
- **`InconclusiveMatchError`** - Flag evaluation inconclusive
- **`RequiresServerEvaluationError`** - Flag needs server evaluation
- **`ErrFlagNotFound`** - Feature flag missing/disabled

## Feature Flags

```go
type FeatureFlagPayload struct {
    Key                   string
    DistinctId            string
    DeviceId              *string
    Groups                Groups
    PersonProperties      Properties
    GroupProperties       map[string]Properties
    OnlyEvaluateLocally   bool
    SendFeatureFlagEvents *bool
}

type FeatureFlagResult struct {
    Key        string
    Enabled    bool
    RawPayload *string
    Variant    *string
}

func (r *FeatureFlagResult) GetPayloadAs(v interface{}) error
```

## Configuration

```go
type Config struct {
    Endpoint                                string
    PersonalApiKey                          string
    DisableGeoIP                            *bool
    Interval                                time.Duration
    DefaultFeatureFlagsPollingInterval      time.Duration
    FeatureFlagRequestTimeout               time.Duration
    Transport                               http.RoundTripper
    Logger                                  Logger
    DefaultEventProperties                  Properties
    Callback                                Callback
    BatchSize                               int
    Verbose                                 bool
    RetryAfter                              func(int) time.Duration
    MaxRetries                              *int
    ShutdownTimeout                         time.Duration
    BatchUploadTimeout                      time.Duration
    BatchSubmitTimeout                      time.Duration
    MaxEnqueuedRequests                     int
    Compression                             CompressionMode
}
```

## Constants

- `SDKName = "posthog-go"`
- `DefaultEndpoint = "https://app.posthog.com"`
- `DefaultInterval = 5 * time.Second`
- `DefaultBatchSize = 250`
- `Version = "1.11.1"`

## Utility Functions

```go
func Ptr[T any](v T) *T  // Helper to make references from values
func NewProperties() Properties
func NewGroups() Groups
func StdLogger(logger *log.Logger, verbose bool) Logger
```

content.text:
Great news — the research shows PostHog covers both sides:

**Go Error Tracking** (posthog-go v1.11.1):
- `NewDefaultException()` — direct exception capture with stack traces
- `SlogCaptureHandler` — wraps Go's `slog`, automatically captures Warn+ as exceptions
- This integrates perfectly with Kapow's existing `slog` logging

**Logs / Operational Metrics** (OpenTelemetry):
- PostHog accepts OTLP logs at `https://us.i.posthog.com/i/v1/logs`
- Standard OpenTelemetry Go SDK — no PostHog-specific packages needed
- Can capture query times, request latency, etc. as structured log attributes

Let me add server-side issues to the epic.

file.content:
# PostHog Integration Plan

## Context

Kapow has zero analytics or error tracking. This plan adds PostHog to the web
frontend for error tracking and product analytics, while architecting the
provider hierarchy so session replay, experiments, and feature flags can be
enabled later without structural changes.

## Approach

Follow the existing `supabase.ts` pattern: a library module that gracefully
degrades to no-ops when env vars are missing. Wrap the app with
`PostHogProvider` from `posthog-js/react` so the `usePostHog()` hook and
future feature-flag hooks work throughout the tree.

## New dependency

- `posthog-js` (includes `posthog-js/react` — provider, hooks)

## Environment variables

| Var | Required | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` | Yes (to enable) | — |
| `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` | No | `https://us.i.posthog.com` |

When `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` is absent, the entire integration is inert.

## Files to create

### 1. `web/src/lib/posthog.ts` — Client initialization

- Read `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` / `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` from `import.meta.env`
- Export `posthogEnabled: boolean`
- If enabled: `posthog.init(key, { api_host, capture_pageview: false, capture_pageleave: true, autocapture: false, respect_dnt: true, persistence: 'localStorage' })`
- Register `window.onerror` / `window.onunhandledrejection` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Export the posthog instance
- If disabled: export a no-op proxy (mirrors supabase.ts pattern)

### 2. `web/src/hooks/usePostHog.ts` — Pageview + identify hooks

- `usePostHogPageview()`: captures `$pageview` on `location.pathname` changes via `useLocation()`
- `usePostHogIdentify()`: subscribes to `useAuthStore`, calls `posthog.identify()` / `posthog.reset()`
- Both are no-ops when `posthogEnabled` is false

### 3. `web/src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx` — React error boundary

- Class component, `componentDidCatch` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Fallback UI using Mantine components (title, message, reload button)

### 4. `web/src/lib/posthogExchange.ts` — urql exchange

- Inspects operation results for `graphQLErrors`
- Captures `graphql_error` events with operation name + error messages
- Passthrough when PostHog is disabled

## Files to modify

### 5. `web/src/main.tsx` — Provider + error boundary wiring

```
StrictMode > UrqlProvider > AppProviders > PostHogProvider > ErrorBoundary > BrowserRouter > App
```

### 6. `web/src/App.tsx` — Wire hooks

- Call `usePostHogPageview()` and `usePostHogIdentify()` in App component

### 7. `web/src/lib/urqlClient.ts` — Add exchange

- Insert `posthogExchange` into exchanges: `[cacheExchange, posthogExchange, authExchange(...), fetchExchange]`

### 8. `web/src/pages/Search.tsx` — Custom analytics events

- `search_executed` — on search results load (filter count, result count, has_location)
- `result_clicked` — on institution click (unit_id, name)
- `export_csv` / `export_pdf` — on export button clicks

### 9. `.env.example` — Document new env vars

### 10. `web/src/test/setup.ts` — Global mocks

- Mock `posthog-js` and `posthog-js/react` with no-op stubs

## PostHog init config

```typescript
posthog.init(key, {
  api_host: host || 'https://us.i.posthog.com',
  capture_pageview: false,       // manual via hook
  capture_pageleave: true,
  autocapture: false,            // explicit events only
  respect_dnt: true,
  persistence: 'localStorage',
  loaded: (ph) => { if (import.meta.env.DEV) ph.debug() },
})
```

## Issues (kapow-d1i)

| Issue | Title | Deps | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| `kapow-d1i.1` | PostHog client module + test mocks | — | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.2` | React error boundary + PostHog exceptions | .1 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.3` | GraphQL error tracking exchange | .1 | P2 |
| `kapow-d1i.4` | Pageview + user identify hooks | .1 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.5` | Search page analytics events | .1 | P2 |
| `kapow-d1i.6` | PostHog provider wiring | .2, .3, .4, .5 | P1 |

**Parallelism**: After `.1` completes, `.2`–`.5` can all run in parallel. `.6` is the final assembly.

## What this intentionally skips

- **Session replay** — enable later by removing `disable_session_recording: true`
- **Feature flags / experiments** — work automatically once enabled in PostHog dashboard; `PostHogProvider` is already in the tree
- **Server-side tracking** — separate concern for the Go API
- **Cookie consent** — US-focused tool; `respect_dnt: true` is sufficient

  api_host: host || 'https://us.i.posthog.com',
  capture_pageview: false,       // manual via hook
  capture_pageleave: true,
  autocapture: false,            // explicit events only
  respect_dnt: true,
  persistence: 'localStorage',
  loaded: (ph) => { if (import.meta.env.DEV) ph.debug() },
})
```

## Issues (kapow-d1i)

| Issue | Title | Deps | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| `kapow-d1i.1` | PostHog client module + test mocks | — | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.2` | React error boundary + PostHog exceptions | .1 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.3` | GraphQL error tracking exchange | .1 | P2 |
| `kapow-d1i.4` | Pageview + user identify hooks | .1 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.5` | Search page analytics events | .1 | P2 |
| `kapow-d1i.6` | PostHog provider wiring | .2, .3, .4, .5 | P1 |

**Parallelism**: After `.1` completes, `.2`–`.5` can all run in parallel. `.6` is the final assembly.

## What this intentionally skips

- **Session replay** — enable later by removing `disable_session_recording: true`
- **Feature flags / experiments** — work automatically once enabled in PostHog dashboard; `PostHogProvider` is already in the tree
- **Server-side tracking** — separate concern for the Go API
- **Cookie consent** — US-focused tool; `respect_dnt: true` is sufficient

## Server-side: Go API error tracking (.7)

### `api/internal/config/config.go` — New env vars

Add `PostHogAPIKey` and `PostHogHost` fields with `env:"POSTHOG_API_KEY"` /
`env:"POSTHOG_HOST"` tags. `PostHogHost` defaults to `https://us.i.posthog.com`.

### `cmd/server/main.go` — PostHog client + slog handler

After pgxpool connect, before router setup:
1. If `cfg.PostHogAPIKey` is set, create `posthog.NewWithConfig(key, config)`
2. Wrap the default slog handler with `posthog.NewSlogCaptureHandler` —
   captures Warn+ as exceptions with stack traces automatically
3. Use `WithDistinctIDFn` to extract user ID from context when available
4. Wire `posthog.Client` into `router.Deps`
5. `defer client.Close()` for flush on shutdown

### `api/internal/router/router.go` — Deps update

Add `PostHog posthog.Client` (or `posthog.EnqueueClient`) to `Deps` struct.
Resolvers can use it for direct exception capture beyond slog.

## Server-side: OpenTelemetry logs (.8)

### Go OTel SDK setup

- Use `go.opentelemetry.io/otel/sdk/log` + `otlploghttp` exporter
- Configure exporter endpoint: `POSTHOG_HOST + /i/v1/logs`
- Auth via project token header
- Create `LoggerProvider`, set as global

### Instrumentation points

- **GraphQL handler**: log query duration as structured attribute
- **Database queries**: middleware/wrapper to log query execution time
- **HTTP middleware**: log request latency per endpoint

## Issues (kapow-d1i)

| Issue | Title | Deps | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| `kapow-d1i.1` | PostHog client module + test mocks | — | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.2` | React error boundary + PostHog exceptions | .1 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.3` | GraphQL error tracking exchange | .1 | P2 |
| `kapow-d1i.4` | Pageview + user identify hooks | .1 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.5` | Search page analytics events | .1 | P2 |
| `kapow-d1i.6` | PostHog provider wiring | .2, .3, .4, .5 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.7` | Go API PostHog error tracking | — | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.8` | OpenTelemetry logs to PostHog | .7 | P2 |

**Parallelism**: Frontend (.1→.2-.5→.6) and API (.7→.8) tracks are independent
and can run in parallel.

## What this intentionally skips

- **Session replay** — enable later by removing `disable_session_recording: true`
- **Feature flags / experiments** — work automatically once enabled in PostHog dashboard; `PostHogProvider` is already in the tree
- **Cookie consent** — US-focused tool; `respect_dnt: true` is sufficient

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# PostHog Integration Plan

## Context

Kapow has zero analytics or error tracking. This plan adds PostHog to both the
web frontend and Go API for error tracking, product analytics, and operational
logging — while architecting for session replay, experiments, and feature flags
to be enabled later without structural changes.

## Approach

Follow the existing `supabase.ts` pattern: a library module that gracefully
degrades to no-ops when env vars are missing. Wrap the app with
`PostHogProvider` from `posthog-js/react` so the `usePostHog()` hook and
future feature-flag hooks work throughout the tree.

## New dependencies

- **Frontend**: `posthog-js` (includes `posthog-js/react` — provider, hooks)
- **API**: `github.com/posthog/posthog-go` v1.11+ (error tracking, slog handler)
- **API**: `go.opentelemetry.io/otel` + OTLP HTTP exporter (structured logs)

## Environment variables

| Var | Side | Required | Default |
|---|---|---|---|
| `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` | Web | Yes (to enable) | — |
| `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` | Web | No | `https://us.i.posthog.com` |
| `POSTHOG_API_KEY` | API | Yes (to enable) | — |
| `POSTHOG_HOST` | API | No | `https://us.i.posthog.com` |

When the key is absent on either side, the integration is inert (no-op).

## Files to create

### 1. `web/src/lib/posthog.ts` — Client initialization

- Read `VITE_POSTHOG_KEY` / `VITE_POSTHOG_HOST` from `import.meta.env`
- Export `posthogEnabled: boolean`
- If enabled: `posthog.init(key, { api_host, capture_pageview: false, capture_pageleave: true, autocapture: false, respect_dnt: true, persistence: 'localStorage' })`
- Register `window.onerror` / `window.onunhandledrejection` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Export the posthog instance
- If disabled: export a no-op proxy (mirrors supabase.ts pattern)

### 2. `web/src/hooks/usePostHog.ts` — Pageview + identify hooks

- `usePostHogPageview()`: captures `$pageview` on `location.pathname` changes via `useLocation()`
- `usePostHogIdentify()`: subscribes to `useAuthStore`, calls `posthog.identify()` / `posthog.reset()`
- Both are no-ops when `posthogEnabled` is false

### 3. `web/src/components/ErrorBoundary.tsx` — React error boundary

- Class component, `componentDidCatch` → `posthog.capture('$exception', ...)`
- Fallback UI using Mantine components (title, message, reload button)

### 4. `web/src/lib/posthogExchange.ts` — urql exchange

- Inspects operation results for `graphQLErrors`
- Captures `graphql_error` events with operation name + error messages
- Passthrough when PostHog is disabled

## Files to modify

### 5. `web/src/main.tsx` — Provider + error boundary wiring

```
StrictMode > UrqlProvider > AppProviders > PostHogProvider > ErrorBoundary > BrowserRouter > App
```

### 6. `web/src/App.tsx` — Wire hooks

- Call `usePostHogPageview()` and `usePostHogIdentify()` in App component

### 7. `web/src/lib/urqlClient.ts` — Add exchange

- Insert `posthogExchange` into exchanges: `[cacheExchange, posthogExchange, authExchange(...), fetchExchange]`

### 8. `web/src/pages/Search.tsx` — Custom analytics events

- `search_executed` — on search results load (filter count, result count, has_location)
- `result_clicked` — on institution click (unit_id, name)
- `export_csv` / `export_pdf` — on export button clicks

### 9. `.env.example` — Document new env vars

### 10. `web/src/test/setup.ts` — Global mocks

- Mock `posthog-js` and `posthog-js/react` with no-op stubs

## PostHog init config

```typescript
posthog.init(key, {
  api_host: host || 'https://us.i.posthog.com',
  capture_pageview: false,       // manual via hook
  capture_pageleave: true,
  autocapture: false,            // explicit events only
  respect_dnt: true,
  persistence: 'localStorage',
  loaded: (ph) => { if (import.meta.env.DEV) ph.debug() },
})
```

## Issues (kapow-d1i)

| Issue | Title | Deps | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| `kapow-d1i.1` | PostHog client module + test mocks | — | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.2` | React error boundary + PostHog exceptions | .1 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.3` | GraphQL error tracking exchange | .1 | P2 |
| `kapow-d1i.4` | Pageview + user identify hooks | .1 | P1 |
| `kapow-d1i.5` | Search page analytics events | .1 | P2 |
| `kapow-d1i.6` | PostHog provider wiring | .2, .3, .4, .5 | P1 |

**Parallelism**: After `.1` completes, `.2`–`.5` can all run in parallel. `.6` is the final assembly.

## What this intentionally skips

- **Session replay** — enable later by removing `disable_session_recording: true`
- **Feature flags / experiments** — work automatically once enabled in PostHog dashboard; `PostHogProvider` is already in the tree
- **Server-side tracking** — separate concern for the Go API
- **Cookie consent** — US-focused tool; `respect_dnt: true` is sufficient
